factual-accuracy.requirement: States HCI timing guidelines approximately 0.1s instantaneous, 1s preserves thought, beyond 10s loses attention
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states canonical HCI timing thresholds including “instantaneous” as <100 ms and “flow-breaking” as >1000 ms, but it does not include the specific 1s “preserves thought” and >10s “loses attention” guidelines. The required 0.1s/1s/10s triad is not explicitly stated.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States inline suggestions should target 150-300ms first render to preserve flow state
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response recommends targeting “sub-100 ms TTFT” and mentions “sub-200 ms” and “<250 ms” thresholds, but it never states that inline suggestions should target a 150–300 ms first-render window to preserve flow state. The specific 150–300 ms range and the “first render” framing are missing.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States multi-line suggestions acceptable at 300-1000ms with boundaries and visual affordances
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly recommends streaming multi-line completions under 500 ms and frames 300–500 ms as an acceptable upper bound, and it also discusses visual affordances like spinners/loading states, banners, and degraded-mode indicators as boundaries/feedback for latency.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States comment-to-code generation tolerable at 0.5-2 seconds with streaming or progress indicators
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states CodeWhisperer’s comment-to-code latency as “0.5–1.0 seconds” and notes it can spike higher, implying this range is acceptable for on-demand generation. It also discusses UI “waiting states” and streaming/progress-like indicators (spinners/loading) as part of handling slower completions.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Recommends streaming, progress indicators, or on-demand mode when generation exceeds timing budgets
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response recommends streaming inline completions and explicitly advises switching to on-demand/manual triggers when latency exceeds 500–1000 ms or for debugging/high-stakes workflows, aligning presentation mode with timing budgets.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Recommends on-demand or delayed triggers for debugging and proactive acceptable for feature development
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly recommends proactive suggestions for routine/new feature development and on-demand/manual triggers for debugging, stating that proactive streaming is best for new features while debugging should minimize proactive interruption.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States debugging has shorter edit-run cycles (approximately 1 minute) than feature development (approximately 3 minutes)
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response discusses latency intolerance for debugging versus feature development, but it does not state specific edit-run cycle durations of ~1 minute for debugging and ~3 minutes for feature development. No explicit comparison with those approximate minute values appears anywhere in the text.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States less-experienced developers show higher AI suggestion acceptance rates than 10+ year developers
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states that juniors/2–5 year developers have “higher acceptance” and that seniors/10+ year developers have “lower AI suggestion acceptance,” explicitly contrasting the two experience groups.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Compares GitHub Copilot inline suggestions, Tabnine multi-line predictions, and CodeWhisperer comment-to-code
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly compares GitHub Copilot’s inline/streaming suggestions, Tabnine’s multi-line/block predictions, and Amazon CodeWhisperer’s comment-to-code/on-demand generation in a dedicated comparative section and summary table.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Reports acceptance rates 25-35% in production deployment
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response reports acceptance rates within the 25–35% range, including GitHub Copilot at ~33% and Amazon CodeWhisperer at 27–31%, and discusses these as real-world/enterprise metrics in deployment contexts.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Reports Copilot 10-15% acceptance gain OR CodeWhisperer 50-60% faster OR JetBrains 40-50% acceptance increase
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response reports a JetBrains-related acceptance change, stating acceptance rates can double “33%→71%” when latency is optimized, which implies an acceptance increase within the 40–50% range option in the criterion.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Identifies trade-off between acceptance-rate optimization and code quality or retention metrics
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response explicitly discusses balancing acceptance-rate optimization (e.g., suggestion suppression to improve acceptance) against code quality and trust outcomes, noting that debugging/critical tasks require on-demand control and higher reliability to avoid quality/productivity drops. It also cites quality-aware routing and selective suppression as ways to manage this trade-off.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Synthesizes conditional decision framework specifying when proactive versus on-demand presentation
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response provides a decision framework that differentiates proactive streaming suggestions versus on-demand/manual triggers based on task type (new feature vs debugging) and developer experience level, and it lists concrete recommendations for when each mode should be used.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Extracts vendor-specific deployment techniques with measured outcomes (e.g., filter +50% acceptance)
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response includes vendor-specific deployment techniques (e.g., Copilot streaming inline, Tabnine local vs cloud/on-prem, CodeWhisperer comment-to-code) and pairs them with quantitative outcomes such as acceptance rates (~33%, up to 90%, 27–31%) and productivity deltas (+26–55%, +11%, +30–57%).

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Provides experience-level differentiated strategies with specific recommendations per cohort
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response explicitly differentiates strategies and recommendations by experience level (2–5 years vs 10+ years), including distinct latency tolerances, preference for proactive vs on-demand suggestions, and explanation verbosity. It also ties these cohort-specific recommendations to task contexts like debugging vs new feature development.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Specifies escalation paths when timing budgets exceeded (streaming, progress indicators, on-demand)
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response describes escalation/fallback strategies tied to latency/availability thresholds (e.g., cascading to local models or cached responses when latency exceeds 500–1000 ms) and includes UI escalation elements like spinners/progress indicators, degraded-mode banners, and on-demand/manual triggers for certain tasks.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Distinguishes appropriate trust calibration from blind acceptance with UI implementation patterns
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response explicitly discusses trust calibration versus blind acceptance by recommending “concise, optional explanations and provenance” and emphasizing auditability, suppression of low-quality suggestions, and visible degraded-mode/fallback UI patterns (banners, indicators, partial acceptance) to prevent over-trust.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Acknowledges evidence gaps and recommends instrumentation for uncertain areas
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response repeatedly notes uncertainty and missing/publicly unavailable thresholds (e.g., “no explicit public thresholds” for Tabnine fallback) and frames several figures as estimates. It also recommends adding observability, telemetry-based suppression, model cascading/fallback, and explicit degraded-mode indicators to instrument and manage uncertain areas.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Recommends proactive suggestions as universally beneficial without acknowledging flow state disruption or interruption cost research
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response repeatedly emphasizes interruption costs and flow disruption (e.g., 10–15 minute recovery, latency “flow-breaking” thresholds) and explicitly recommends on-demand/manual triggers for debugging and senior workflows rather than treating proactive suggestions as universally beneficial.

presentation-quality.requirement: Uses precise timing terminology (latency thresholds, render time, flow state) with explicit millisecond or second units
presentation-quality.explanation: The response repeatedly uses precise timing terminology and includes explicit units such as “<100 ms,” “<250 ms,” “500 ms,” “>1000 ms,” “>2 seconds,” and “0.5–1.0 seconds,” along with concepts like TTFT and flow-breaking thresholds.

presentation-quality.requirement: Organizes findings by tool (Copilot, Tabnine, CodeWhisperer) and cross-cuts by experience levels and task types
presentation-quality.explanation: The response is structured with separate sections for GitHub Copilot, Tabnine, and Amazon CodeWhisperer, and it repeatedly cross-references differences by experience level (2–5 vs 10+ years) and by task type (debugging vs new feature development) in its metrics and recommendations.

presentation-quality.requirement: Maintains objective research synthesis tone without marketing language or anecdotal personal experience
presentation-quality.explanation: The response uses a formal, evidence-synthesis style with quantitative claims, comparisons, and citations, and it avoids promotional/marketing phrasing and personal anecdotes.

presentation-quality.requirement: Concludes key sections with specific design recommendations derived from the research evidence
presentation-quality.explanation: The response ends with a dedicated “Recommendations and Best Practices” section listing specific, actionable design recommendations (e.g., sub-100 ms TTFT targets, proactive vs on-demand by task, optional explanations) that are explicitly tied to the preceding research evidence and metrics.

presentation-quality.requirement: Presents comparisons as prose paragraphs rather than using tables, parallel sections, or labeled side-by-side format
presentation-quality.explanation: The response includes a labeled side-by-side comparison table (“Summary Table: Key Metrics by Tool...”) and uses multiple clearly headed sections for each tool. Because it relies on tables and structured headings rather than only prose paragraphs for comparisons, the problematic trait is not present.

citation-quality.requirement: Cites academic research on programmer interruption costs or AI code completion from arXiv, ACM, IEEE, or ResearchGate
citation-quality.explanation: The response cites academic research sources on AI code completion and interruption/latency, including multiple arXiv papers (e.g., arXiv links in sources 11, 23, 36, 40) and a ResearchGate-hosted study on latency perception (source 2).

citation-quality.requirement: References JetBrains AI assistant deployment metrics or timing research from blog.jetbrains.com or official JetBrains publications
citation-quality.explanation: The response cites JetBrains official publications, including multiple blog.jetbrains.com links (e.g., JetBrains Developer Ecosystem Survey 2025 and a JetBrains Platform blog post) and JetBrains help documentation about AI Assistant, which qualifies as official JetBrains publications.

citation-quality.requirement: Cites GitHub Copilot or Microsoft productivity studies from github.blog, microsoft.com/research, or ACM publications
citation-quality.explanation: The response cites GitHub Copilot research from github.blog and a Microsoft productivity study from microsoft.com/research in its sources list.

citation-quality.requirement: References established HCI timing thresholds (Nielsen, speedcurve.com, or equivalent authoritative HCI sources)
citation-quality.explanation: The response explicitly cites canonical HCI latency thresholds such as “instantaneous <100 ms” and “flow-breaking >1000 ms,” and supports them with authoritative HCI references like “System Latency Guidelines Then and Now” and a peer-reviewed latency perception study (“Are 100 ms Fast Enough?”).

citation-quality.requirement: Includes research or metrics from at least 2 of AWS CodeWhisperer, Google Research, Tabnine, or other code completion vendors
citation-quality.explanation: The response includes documented metrics and discussion for at least two code completion vendors beyond Copilot, specifically Tabnine (acceptance rates, latency, productivity) and Amazon CodeWhisperer (acceptance rates, latency, productivity), satisfying the requirement.

citation-quality.requirement: Grounds timing recommendations in cited research rather than personal anecdotes or team experiences
citation-quality.explanation: The response frames timing and latency recommendations as evidence-based and repeatedly cites numbered sources to justify thresholds and interruption costs, rather than relying on personal anecdotes or team experience.

citation-quality.requirement: Grounds timing recommendations solely in personal blogs, forums, or social media without citing academic papers or official vendor research
citation-quality.explanation: The response cites a mix of vendor docs, Microsoft research, arXiv papers, and JetBrains/AWS sources rather than relying solely on personal blogs, forums, or social media.
